ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for stir-fried pork with vegetables; closest parent 'pork dish' (FOODON_00002764) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific term for stir-fried pork with vegetables; 'Pig meat, cooked' (A0CKP) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT food concept found for stir-fried pork with vegetables dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising roughly 150 g of stir-fried pork with vegetables and 200 g of steamed white rice, as typically served in a Singapore hawker or casual dining setting.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, stir-fried with vegetables — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central composite estimate for stir-fried pork with mixed vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo does not list 'nong jia xiao chao rou' by exact name. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore nutrient data for stir-fried pork dishes and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked pork with vegetables (USDA SR Legacy: Pork, fresh, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked; and mixed vegetable stir-fry composites). Per-100g values represent the pork-and-vegetable component only, excluding rice.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed pork and vegetable stir-fry with soy sauce
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nong jia xiao chao rou. GI of 48 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed pork and vegetable stir-fry dishes with soy-based sauce, which are classified as low-GI foods due to the predominance of protein and fat over carbohydrate. The carbohydrate contribution from the pork-and-vegetable component alone is modest (approximately 5.2 g per 100 g). Glycemic load per serving (350 g total plate including 200 g rice) is calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (48 × 26) / 100 ≈ 12.5, where available carbohydrate per serving accounts for both the pork-vegetable component (~7.8 g) and the rice component (~45 g at GI 72, blended estimate). This is a composite analogous estimate and should be updated if direct measurement becomes available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce preparation, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (exceeding 1,800 mg per full serving with rice) significantly contributes to daily sodium targets of under 2,000 mg recommended by HPB Singapore.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load when served with white rice; protein and fat content attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that while the pork-and-vegetable component has a low glycemic index (estimated GI 48), the full plate served with white rice raises the overall glycemic load substantially; substituting brown rice or reducing rice portion can help manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and elevated sodium from soy-based sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption of this dish due to its saturated fat content (approximately 3.9 g per 100 g) and high sodium load; choosing leaner pork cuts and requesting lighter sauce can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Pork with Vegetables (农家小炒肉, Nóng jiā xiǎo chǎo ròu) is a popular Chinese and Chinese Singaporean dish featuring thinly sliced pork — typically pork belly or pork shoulder — rapidly stir-fried in a wok over high heat with a medley of vegetables such as chili peppers, garlic, ginger, and seasonal greens. The dish is seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sometimes fermented black bean paste, producing a savory, slightly spicy, and umami-rich flavor profile. The texture contrasts tender, slightly caramelized pork slices with crisp-tender vegetables. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a rice dish (农家小炒肉饭) at Chinese hawker stalls and casual restaurants, including establishments such as Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles. Nutritionally, the pork-and-vegetable component provides approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with 14.5 g protein, 11.8 g fat, and 5.2 g carbohydrate. The dish is a good source of B vitamins, zinc, and iron from pork, and provides vitamin C and dietary fibre from vegetables. Clinically, the primary concern is its high sodium content from soy-based sauces, making it a moderate-to-high concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the pork-and-vegetable component is estimated at 48 (low), though the full meal glycemic load increases substantially when served with white rice. It is not suitable for halal or kosher diets due to pork content.

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry found for nong jia xiao chao rou by name. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore stir-fried pork dish data and USDA FoodData Central cooked pork composites. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar stir-fried pork and vegetable dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of a direct named match in a primary approved source.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases consulted for this dish type; pork is a minor dietary source of vitamin D and the value is not reliably established for this preparation. 2. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork composite data and are approximate. 3. The food is served as a rice plate (农家小炒肉饭) at the source restaurant; per-100g values represent the pork-and-vegetable stir-fry component only, not the full plate with rice. 4. Glycemic load per serving (12.5) is a composite estimate for the full plate including 200 g steamed white rice. 5. This dish contains pork and is not suitable for halal, kosher, or vegetarian/vegan diets. 6. Sodium content is high due to soy sauce and oyster sauce; individuals on sodium-restricted diets should exercise caution. 7. The field dataed in the source data (Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles 福记红烧牛肉面 - Lorong Telok) is a Chinese noodle restaurant in Singapore that also serves rice dishes including this item. 8. GI category 'low' is confirmed: glycemic_index = 48, which is ≤ 55 per Veritas threshold rules.
